questions.question: Fair value can swing by σ = 2 over your quoting horizon. You set the half-spread to cover ±2σ. What total spread width does that imply?

explanations.C: Correct. half-spread = 2σ = 2×2 = 4; total spread = 2 × 4 = 8.

explanations.D: 12 over-covers — that is 1.5× the required width.

questions.question: Fair value can swing by σ = 4 over your quoting horizon. You set the half-spread to cover ±2σ. What total spread width does that imply?

explanations.A: Correct. half-spread = 2σ = 2×4 = 8; total spread = 2 × 8 = 16.
explanations.B: 8 is just the half-spread (one side); the full width is twice that.
explanations.C: 4 is one σ, not the spread implied by ±2σ coverage.
explanations.D: 24 over-covers — that is 1.5× the required width.

questions.question: Fair value can swing by σ = 1.5 over your quoting horizon. You set the half-spread to cover ±4σ. What total spread width does that imply?

explanations.B: Correct. half-spread = 4σ = 4×1.5 = 6; total spread = 2 × 6 = 12.
explanations.A: 6 is just the half-spread (one side); the full width is twice that.
explanations.C: 1.5 is one σ, not the spread implied by ±4σ coverage.
explanations.D: 18 over-covers — that is 1.5× the required width.

questions.question: In Larry Harris's framework, the bid-ask spread compensates the market maker for three costs. Which set names them?

explanations.A: these are business overheads, not the microstructure costs the spread is modeled to cover.
explanations.B: these are trading frictions but not Harris's three spread components.
explanations.C: Correct. Harris attributes the spread to processing fills, carrying inventory risk, and being picked off by informed traders.
explanations.D: these describe market conditions, not the cost components of the spread.

questions.question: An informed trader buys from you precisely when your ask is below the true value. This is an example of:

explanations.A: inventory risk is about holding a position, not about being traded against on mispriced quotes.
explanations.B: that is the fixed cost of handling a trade, not being picked off.
explanations.C: being lifted below value is a loss, not the edge you earn when centered.
explanations.D: Correct. informed flow trades against you when your quote is stale or wrong, so you transact on the losing side.

questions.question: Your uncertainty about fair value suddenly increases. All else equal, your spread should:

explanations.A: Correct. a wider spread charges more edge to compensate for the greater chance of being wrong.
explanations.B: tightening takes on more risk with less compensation — the wrong response to rising uncertainty.
explanations.C: a good quote adapts to conditions; ignoring higher uncertainty invites pick-offs.
explanations.D: a zero spread removes all compensation exactly when risk is highest.

questions.question: You are extremely confident in fair value with very little estimation error. A reasonable response is to:

explanations.A: wide spreads are for high uncertainty; here you would just lose flow for no reason.
explanations.B: Correct. low uncertainty means little adverse-selection risk to charge for, so you can compete on a tighter spread.
explanations.C: confidence is a reason to quote, not to withdraw.
explanations.D: a crossed quote (bid ≥ ask) is always invalid regardless of confidence.

questions.question: Which of the following two-sided quotes is crossed (and therefore invalid)?

explanations.A: this is a normal quote with bid below ask.
explanations.B: this is locked (zero spread), not crossed.
explanations.C: Correct. the bid (103) is above the ask (99); a crossed quote is invalid.
explanations.D: this is a normal quote with a positive spread.

questions.question: A 'crossed' market specifically means:
options: Bid is less than the ask
Bid is greater than the ask

explanations.A: that is a normal, valid market.
explanations.B: bid = ask is locked, not crossed.
explanations.C: uncertainty about fair value does not by itself cross a quote.
explanations.D: Correct. when the bid exceeds the ask the quote is inverted and cannot stand.

questions.question: Fair value jumps from 60 up to 70. Your resting quote is 65 at 67. The best immediate action is to:
options: Re-center near 70 with a sensible spread (e.g. 68 at 72)

explanations.A: Correct. updating toward the new fair value avoids selling cheap and being picked off.
explanations.B: your ask of 67 is now far below fair 70 — you will be lifted and lose.
explanations.C: that is crossed (bid above ask) and invalid.
explanations.D: you may pause briefly, but the skill is re-centering, not quitting the market.

questions.question: When the consensus fair value rises, a disciplined market maker most often:
options: Raises both bid and ask to track the new fair
Only widens without moving the center

explanations.A: that moves quotes the wrong way, deeper into being picked off.
explanations.B: Correct. shifting the whole quote up keeps it centered on the updated value.
explanations.C: widening alone leaves a stale midpoint below fair.
explanations.D: you generally keep quoting both sides.

questions.question: Alice quotes 97 at 103 (spread 6) and Bob quotes 99 at 101 (spread 2) on the same fair-100 product. Comparing them:
options: Bob earns more per round-trip and is better protected
They earn identical edge per round-trip
Alice earns more gross edge per round-trip but Bob is more exposed to a fair-value shock on a one-sided fill

explanations.A: Bob's spread is narrower, so he earns less and has a thinner cushion.
explanations.B: their spreads differ (6 vs 2), so round-trip edge differs.
explanations.C: Correct. Alice's wider spread earns 6 vs Bob's 2, while Bob's 1-point cushion offers less protection if fair moves.
explanations.D: Alice's wider spread is precisely what cushions her against shocks.

questions.question: Holding fair value fixed, a wider spread (versus a tighter one) generally gives you:
options: Less edge per round-trip and more frequent fills
More edge and more fills simultaneously
No effect on edge per round-trip
More edge per round-trip but potentially fewer fills

explanations.A: that describes a tighter spread, not a wider one.
explanations.B: you cannot generally maximize both; width trades flow for edge.
explanations.C: the round-trip edge equals the spread, so width matters directly.
explanations.D: Correct. a wider spread captures more per trade but is less competitive, so it trades less often.

questions.question: A hidden card will be revealed in 10 seconds and could move fair value by ±15. You are currently quoting 49 at 51. The most defensible adjustment is to:
options: Widen materially (e.g. 45 at 55) while staying centered on 50
Tighten to 49.5 at 50.5 to attract flow before the reveal
Keep 49 at 51 because the spread already compensates
Quote a one-sided bid only at 50

explanations.A: Correct. large imminent information risk warrants a wider spread to cover pick-off risk while remaining near fair.
explanations.B: tightening before a volatility event invites informed traders to hit the wrong side.
explanations.C: a 2-point spread is tiny against a ±15 swing — you would be picked off.
explanations.D: the priority is two-sided protection by widening, not removing a side.

questions.question: Realized volatility over your quoting horizon doubles, but everything else is unchanged. To keep the same coverage in σ-units, your spread should:

explanations.A: an unchanged spread would now cover only half as many σ — less protection.
explanations.B: Correct. if the spread is set as a multiple of σ, doubling σ doubles the width needed for equal coverage.
explanations.C: halving the spread when volatility rises increases pick-off risk sharply.
explanations.D: zero spread offers no compensation, worst exactly when volatility is high.

questions.question: Why is the spread NOT 'guaranteed profit' on every fill?
options: Because exchanges forbid profits on fills
Because the spread is always negative
Because informed traders fill you when your quote is wrong, so some fills lose money
Because you can only ever buy, never sell

explanations.A: there is no such rule; the issue is economic, not regulatory.
explanations.B: the spread is positive by construction (ask > bid).
explanations.C: Correct. adverse selection means you only earn the spread on average, not on every individual trade.
explanations.D: market makers quote and trade both sides.

questions.question: Two quotes have the same width, but quote X is centered on fair value and quote Y sits 5 points off fair. Which is more likely to get picked off?
options: Neither — width is all that matters

explanations.A: a centered quote is balanced around fair and is the safer of the two.
explanations.B: calibration matters as much as width; a mis-centered quote is exposed regardless of width.
explanations.C: the off-center quote is clearly more exposed on its mispriced side.
explanations.D: Correct. an off-center quote presents one side far from fair, which informed traders will hit.

questions.question: In a dice-game setting, your classmates trade with you mostly when your quote is stale. The spread you charge is best understood as:
options: Payment you collect for bearing adverse-selection and inventory risk

explanations.A: Correct. the spread is the edge that, on average, offsets being picked off and carrying inventory.
explanations.B: it is not an external fee; it is your compensation for risk.
explanations.C: there is real risk; stale-quote pick-offs are losses.
explanations.D: the spread is the core driver of a market maker's edge.

questions.question: You are flat (no inventory) and confident fair value is 100. Which is the most disciplined two-sided quote?

explanations.A: a zero spread captures no edge and gives no cushion.
explanations.B: Correct. a tight, symmetric quote around fair is appropriate when you are confident and flat.
explanations.C: that is needlessly wide given high confidence and no inventory pressure.
explanations.D: that quote is crossed (bid above ask) and invalid.

questions.question: You have accumulated a large long position and are nervous about it. A natural quoting adjustment is to:
options: Skew your quote up to buy even more
Skew your quote down (lower bid and ask) to encourage selling out of the long

explanations.A: that would add to an already uncomfortable long position.
explanations.B: widening alone does not address the directional inventory imbalance.
explanations.C: Correct. shifting the quote down makes you more likely to sell and less likely to buy, reducing the unwanted long.
explanations.D: a crossed quote is invalid and does not manage inventory.

questions.question: The gross edge from one completed round-trip (buy the bid, sell the ask, fair unchanged) equals:

explanations.A: half the spread is the per-side edge, not the full round-trip.
explanations.B: that double-counts; the round-trip earns the spread once.
explanations.C: zero ignores the captured spread when fair value does not move.
explanations.D: Correct. buying at the bid and selling at the ask captures the entire bid-ask difference.

questions.question: On a single one-sided fill at your quote (no offsetting trade), the edge you earn relative to fair value is approximately:

explanations.A: Correct. one side of a symmetric quote sits half a spread from fair, so a single fill earns about the half-spread.
explanations.B: the full spread requires completing both legs, not one.
explanations.C: that overstates a single-leg fill by a factor of four.
explanations.D: if fair is unchanged, filling on your quoted side is profitable versus fair.

questions.question: If you quote a bid of 73 and an ask of 73 (bid equals ask), your market is best described as:

explanations.A: crossed means bid above ask; here they are equal.
explanations.B: Correct. equal bid and ask is a locked market with no spread to capture.
explanations.C: there is no positive spread when bid equals ask.
explanations.D: a locked market is possible, just not profitable on the spread.

questions.question: You quote spread in 'units of uncertainty.' A product with twice the fair-value uncertainty of another, all else equal, should be quoted with:

explanations.A: equal spreads ignore the doubled uncertainty and underprice risk.
explanations.B: tightening on a more uncertain product increases pick-off risk.
explanations.C: Correct. more uncertainty calls for more spread to compensate for the larger chance of being wrong.
explanations.D: the issue is width, not removing a side.

questions.question: Before a scheduled announcement that could sharply move fair value, the prudent move for a market maker is usually to:
options: Tighten spreads to grab pre-event flow
Widen spreads (or briefly pull quotes) ahead of the event

explanations.A: tightening into known volatility maximizes the chance of being run over.
explanations.B: a zero spread removes all protection right before a shock.
explanations.C: ignoring known event risk is how stale quotes get picked off.
explanations.D: Correct. uncertainty spikes around announcements, so charging more edge or stepping back limits pick-off risk.

questions.question: A spread that looks tight on paper but is centered far from fair value will most likely:
options: Get picked off on its mispriced side
Be safer than a wide centered quote
Have no risk because it is tight

explanations.A: Correct. informed traders hit the side that is off fair, turning the 'tight' quote into a loss.
explanations.B: being mis-centered means one side is exposed, so edge is not steady.
explanations.C: a wide, well-centered quote is generally safer than a tight, mis-centered one.
explanations.D: tightness without correct centering is dangerous, not safe.

questions.question: Round-trip costs (fees and slippage) exceed your spread. The net edge per round-trip is:

explanations.A: if costs exceed the spread, the gross edge cannot stay positive after costs.
explanations.B: Correct. when costs are larger than the spread captured, the round-trip loses money.
explanations.C: zero only happens when costs equal the spread, not when they exceed it.
explanations.D: the spread is gross; net subtracts the (larger) costs.

questions.question: All else equal, lowering your spread tends to:
options: Increase both fill frequency and edge per fill
Increase fill frequency but reduce edge per fill

explanations.A: you cannot generally raise both; tightening trades edge for flow.
explanations.B: a tighter quote is more competitive, so it usually fills more, not less.
explanations.C: Correct. a tighter, more competitive quote trades more often but earns less on each trade.
explanations.D: spread directly drives both edge per fill and competitiveness.

questions.question: A higher proportion of informed traders in your flow should push you to quote:
options: Wider, to charge more for pick-off risk

explanations.A: more informed flow means more pick-offs; tightening makes the problem worse.
explanations.B: ignoring a higher informed share underprices the risk.
explanations.C: zero spread maximizes losses to informed flow.
explanations.D: Correct. a larger informed share raises adverse-selection cost, which a wider spread compensates.

questions.question: Inventory-holding cost in the spread reflects:
options: The risk of carrying a position whose value can move before you offset it
The fee an exchange charges per share
The guaranteed profit on each fill

explanations.A: Correct. holding inventory exposes you to price moves, and the spread compensates for that risk.
explanations.B: that is an explicit transaction fee, not inventory risk.
explanations.C: there is no guaranteed profit; inventory carries risk.
explanations.D: the midpoint is a price, not a cost component.

questions.question: Two products have identical fair values, but Product A has stable value and Product B is highly volatile. You should quote:
options: A wider spread on A and tighter on B
A tighter spread on A and a wider spread on B

explanations.A: that reverses the risk relationship and underprices the volatile product.
explanations.B: Correct. the volatile product carries more risk, so it earns a wider spread; the stable one can be tighter.
explanations.C: identical spreads ignore the difference in risk between the products.
explanations.D: zero spread on the riskier product invites the most pick-offs.

questions.question: You realize your quote midpoint has drifted 4 points below the true fair value. The most likely consequence if you do nothing is:
options: Sellers hit your bid and you profit
Nothing, because spread alone protects you
Buyers lift your (too-low) ask, and you sell below fair

explanations.A: with the quote too low, the danger is your ask, not a windfall on the bid.
explanations.B: spread does not protect a quote that is centered away from fair.
explanations.C: Correct. a midpoint below fair leaves the ask cheap, so informed buyers pick you off on the sell side.
explanations.D: inventory does not self-correct; mispricing just gets exploited.

questions.question: Which statement best captures the spread trade-off a market maker manages?
options: Always quote the widest possible spread
Always quote the tightest possible spread
Spread choice does not matter if you are fast
Quote tight enough to trade yet wide enough to survive adverse selection

explanations.A: the widest spread wins almost no flow, so it forgoes volume and edge.
explanations.B: the tightest spread maximizes pick-off risk and minimizes edge per trade.
explanations.C: speed helps, but spread choice directly determines edge and protection.
explanations.D: Correct. the craft is balancing competitiveness for flow against protection from being picked off.

questions.question: A market maker says 'I think in spread per unit of volatility.' The practical upshot is that, as volatility rises, the maker:
options: Scales the spread up roughly in proportion to volatility
Keeps a fixed dollar spread regardless of volatility
Narrows the spread as volatility rises
Sets the spread purely from inventory and ignores volatility

explanations.A: Correct. expressing the spread as a multiple of σ means higher volatility mechanically widens the quoted width.
explanations.B: a fixed spread covers fewer σ as volatility rises, leaving the maker under-protected.
explanations.C: narrowing into higher volatility increases the chance of being run over.
explanations.D: volatility is a primary input to spread width, not something to ignore.
